old_claim.note: Herbalism provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct herbalism prerequisite remains because this node is scoped to monastic communities and manuscript-preserving scriptoria, while herbal medicine belongs in downstream hospital, infirmary, and early-medicine nodes.

source_shape.source_locator: The entry defines a scriptorium as a medieval monastic writing room for copying and illuminating manuscripts, with discussion centered on book production and manuscript preservation rather than herbal medicine.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source supports a manuscript-copying scope for scriptoria and does not support herbalism as a direct prerequisite.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Herbal practice may be part of some monastic medical activity, but keeping it direct launders infirmary and hospital context into the scriptoria/book-preservation node.

ontology_before: Monasticism and Scriptoria depended directly on Herbalism, making medicinal practice part of the prerequisite set for manuscript-preserving monastic institutions.
ontology_after: Monasticism and Scriptoria depends on writing, codex book form, philosophy, and agriculture; herbalism remains available for medical descendants rather than this manuscript-production scope.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: herbalism is no longer a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to monastic infirmaries and herb gardens rather than scriptoria.
A stronger source showed herbal medical practice was required for the represented monastic/scriptoria institution.
Downstream hospital nodes lost their direct medical-practice prerequisites.
short_reason: Herbalism is medical context, not a scriptoria prerequisite.
